title: Spring MVC vs WebFlux
summary: Threading models, backpressure, when to use reactive, and how WebFlux handles concurrency differently from MVC.

sections.content:
## Threading Models

### Spring MVC (Servlet Stack)
- **Thread-per-request** model
- Each request gets a thread from the servlet container pool (default: 200 threads in Tomcat)
- Thread blocks waiting for DB, HTTP calls, etc.
- Scales to ~200-500 concurrent requests

### Spring WebFlux (Reactive Stack)
- **Event loop** model (like Node.js)
- Small number of threads (usually = CPU cores)
- Threads never block — tasks are registered as callbacks
- Scales to thousands of concurrent requests with same thread count

sections.title: MVC vs WebFlux Threading Model
sections.content:
graph TB
    subgraph Spring MVC - Thread per Request
        R1[Request 1] --> T1[Thread 1 BLOCKED]
        R2[Request 2] --> T2[Thread 2 BLOCKED]
        R3[Request 3] --> T3[Thread 3 BLOCKED]
        RN[Request N] --> TN[Thread N - pool exhausted!]
    end
    subgraph Spring WebFlux - Event Loop
        EL[Event Loop\n4 threads] --> |non-blocking| W1[Request 1]
        EL --> |non-blocking| W2[Request 2]
        EL --> |non-blocking| W3[1000 requests!]
    end
    style TN fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style EL fill:#059669,color:#fff

## Reactive Types: Mono and Flux

```java
// Mono: 0 or 1 item
Mono<User> user = userRepo.findById(id); // async single result

// Flux: 0 to N items
Flux<User> users = userRepo.findAll(); // async stream

// Chaining (like CompletableFuture but richer)
Mono<UserProfile> profile = userRepo.findById(id)
    .flatMap(user -> orderRepo.findByUserId(user.getId())
        .collectList()
        .map(orders -> new UserProfile(user, orders)))
    .switchIfEmpty(Mono.error(new NotFoundException()));
```

### Key operators:
| Operator | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `map()` | Transform item |
| `flatMap()` | Async transform (returns Mono/Flux) |
| `filter()` | Filter items |
| `zipWith()` | Combine two publishers |
| `switchIfEmpty()` | Fallback if empty |
| `onErrorResume()` | Error recovery |

sections.title: Reactive Pipeline with Backpressure
sections.content:
graph LR
    DB[Database\nFlux<Row>] -->|produces| Proc[Processing\noperators]
    Proc -->|produces| Client[HTTP Client]
    Client -->|demand signal| Proc
    Proc -->|demand signal| DB
    Note[Backpressure: consumer controls\nhow fast producer sends]
    style DB fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style Client fill:#059669,color:#fff

## Backpressure

Backpressure is the ability for a **consumer to signal how fast it can consume** to the producer. This prevents out-of-memory errors when producers are faster than consumers.

```java
// Without backpressure — producer overwhelms consumer
Flux.range(1, 1_000_000)
    .subscribe(item -> slowProcess(item)); // might OOM

// With backpressure — consumer requests items it can handle
Flux.range(1, 1_000_000)
    .limitRate(100)  // request 100 at a time
    .subscribe(item -> slowProcess(item));
```

sections.title: When to Use MVC vs WebFlux
Don't block in WebFlux
sections.content: Never call blocking code (JDBC, Thread.sleep, .block()) on a WebFlux event loop thread. It will block ALL requests on that thread. If you must use blocking I/O, offload to a separate Scheduler: Mono.fromCallable(() -> blockingCall()).subscribeOn(Schedulers.boundedElastic())

sections.question: What is backpressure in reactive programming?
options: The CPU pressure caused by reactive transformations
The ability for a consumer to signal to a producer how fast it can consume data
A technique to buffer data before sending to clients
The overhead of creating Mono and Flux objects
sections.explanation: Backpressure is a flow control mechanism where the consumer signals to the producer how much data it can handle. This prevents slow consumers from being overwhelmed by fast producers, avoiding out-of-memory errors and maintaining system stability.
